examples.explanation: n=5. Initialize result=[], current_sum=0. i=1: is_prime(1)? Check divisibility from 2 to √1=1 → no divisors, but 1 is not prime by definition → not prime. Append current_sum=0 → result=[0]. i=2: is_prime(2)? Check from 2 to √2≈1.41 → only check 2, 2%2==0 → prime. current_sum=0+2=2. Append 2 → result=[0,2]. i=3: is_prime(3)? Check from 2 to √3≈1.73 → check 2, 3%2=1 → prime. current_sum=2+3=5. Append 5 → result=[0,2,5]. i=4: is_prime(4)? Check from 2 to √4=2 → check 2, 4%2=0 → not prime. Append current_sum=5 → result=[0,2,5,5]. i=5: is_prime(5)? Check from 2 to √5≈2.23 → check 2, 5%2=1; check 3? 3>√5 so stop → prime. current_sum=5+5=10. Append 10 → result=[0,2,5,5,10]. Final output [0,2,5,5,10].

n=10. Initialize result=[], current_sum=0. i=1: not prime → append 0 → [0]. i=2: prime → current_sum=2 → append 2 → [0,2]. i=3: prime → current_sum=5 → append 5 → [0,2,5]. i=4: not prime → append 5 → [0,2,5,5]. i=5: prime → current_sum=10 → append 10 → [0,2,5,5,10]. i=6: not prime → append 10 → [0,2,5,5,10,10]. i=7: prime → current_sum=17 → append 17 → [0,2,5,5,10,10,17]. i=8: not prime → append 17 → [0,2,5,5,10,10,17,17]. i=9: not prime → append 17 → [0,2,5,5,10,10,17,17,17]. i=10: not prime → append 17 → [0,2,5,5,10,10,17,17,17,17]. Final output [0,2,5,5,10,10,17,17,17,17].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n² log n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through numbers up to n, check each for primality using trial division, and accumulate sums. Use this for small n or as a baseline to understand the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Prime Prefix Sums Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Given an integer n, compute the prefix sum array where each element is the sum of all prime numbers up to that index.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires generating a list where the i-th element (1-indexed) contains the sum of all prime numbers ≤ i. Using the brute force O(n² log n) approach, we iterate through each number from 1 to n, check if it's prime using trial division, and accumulate the sum. The intuition is straightforward: we need to identify primes and maintain a running total. For each i from 1 to n, we test primality by checking divisibility from 2 to √i. If i is prime, we add it to the current sum; otherwise, we carry forward the previous sum. This results in an array where result[i] = sum of primes ≤ i. The time complexity is O(n² log n) because for each of n numbers, we perform up to √i divisions (O(√n) on average), and checking divisibility involves logarithmic factors in bit operations. Space complexity is O(1) excluding output storage. This approach is inefficient for large n but serves as a clear baseline. Connection to L1: This uses the is_prime helper function from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in array indexing, inefficient primality checks (checking up to i instead of √i), and not handling edge cases like n=0 or n=1. Real code pattern: Initialize an empty list result and a variable current_sum = 0. Loop i from 1 to n: if is_prime(i): current_sum += i; append current_sum to result. Return result.

examples.trace: n=5. Initialize result=[], current_sum=0. i=1: is_prime(1)? Check divisibility from 2 to √1=1 → no divisors, but 1 is not prime by definition → not prime. Append current_sum=0 → result=[0]. i=2: is_prime(2)? Check from 2 to √2≈1.41 → only check 2, 2%2==0 → prime. current_sum=0+2=2. Append 2 → result=[0,2]. i=3: is_prime(3)? Check from 2 to √3≈1.73 → check 2, 3%2=1 → prime. current_sum=2+3=5. Append 5 → result=[0,2,5]. i=4: is_prime(4)? Check from 2 to √4=2 → check 2, 4%2=0 → not prime. Append current_sum=5 → result=[0,2,5,5]. i=5: is_prime(5)? Check from 2 to √5≈2.23 → check 2, 5%2=1; check 3? 3>√5 so stop → prime. current_sum=5+5=10. Append 10 → result=[0,2,5,5,10]. Final output [0,2,5,5,10].

n=10. Initialize result=[], current_sum=0. i=1: not prime → append 0 → [0]. i=2: prime → current_sum=2 → append 2 → [0,2]. i=3: prime → current_sum=5 → append 5 → [0,2,5]. i=4: not prime → append 5 → [0,2,5,5]. i=5: prime → current_sum=10 → append 10 → [0,2,5,5,10]. i=6: not prime → append 10 → [0,2,5,5,10,10]. i=7: prime → current_sum=17 → append 17 → [0,2,5,5,10,10,17]. i=8: not prime → append 17 → [0,2,5,5,10,10,17,17]. i=9: not prime → append 17 → [0,2,5,5,10,10,17,17,17]. i=10: not prime → append 17 → [0,2,5,5,10,10,17,17,17,17]. Final output [0,2,5,5,10,10,17,17,17,17].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Trial Division Primality Test
ladders.desc: Check if a given integer is prime by testing divisibility up to its square root.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine determines whether a number is prime using trial division. The intuition is that if a number has any divisor other than 1 and itself, it is composite. By checking divisibility only up to the square root, we optimize because any larger factor would pair with a smaller factor already tested. Step-by-step: For an input number x, if x < 2, return False (not prime). For i from 2 to √x (inclusive), if x % i == 0, return False. If no divisors found, return True. Time complexity is O(√x) for each test, as we perform up to √x divisions. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called for each i in the main loop to build the prefix sums. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases (x=0,1,2), checking up to x instead of √x (inefficient), and incorrect integer square root calculation. Real code pattern: def is_prime(x): if x < 2: return False; for i in range(2, int(x**0.5)+1): if x % i == 0: return False; return True.

examples.trace: x=5. Check if x<2? 5≥2 → proceed. Compute √5≈2.236, so check i from 2 to 2 (inclusive). i=2: 5%2=1 ≠0. Loop ends. No divisors found, return True.
x=4. Check if x<2? 4≥2 → proceed. Compute √4=2, check i from 2 to 2. i=2: 4%2=0 → divisor found, return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Accumulating Sums Conditionally
ladders.desc: Maintain a running sum that updates only when a prime number is encountered.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves iterating through a sequence and conditionally adding to a cumulative sum. The intuition is to build a prefix sum array where each entry reflects the total so far, but only including specific elements (primes). Step-by-step: Initialize an empty list for results and a variable current_sum = 0. For each number i in the range 1 to n, if i is prime (using the primality test), add i to current_sum. Append current_sum to the result list after each iteration. This creates a list where result[i-1] = sum of primes ≤ i. Time complexity depends on the primality checks; for this sub-problem assuming is_prime is given, it's O(n) for the loop. Space complexity is O(1) for variables, plus O(n) for output. Connection to L1: This uses the is_prime function to decide when to update the sum. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indexing, not initializing current_sum correctly, or appending before updating the sum. Real code pattern: result = []; current_sum = 0; for i in range(1, n+1): if is_prime(i): current_sum += i; result.append(current_sum); return result.

examples.input: n=4, primes=[2,3] (i.e., is_prime returns True for 2 and 3 only)
examples.trace: Given n=4 and is_prime identifies primes as 2 and 3. Initialize result=[], current_sum=0. i=1: is_prime(1)=False → current_sum=0, append 0 → result=[0]. i=2: is_prime(2)=True → current_sum=0+2=2, append 2 → result=[0,2]. i=3: is_prime(3)=True → current_sum=2+3=5, append 5 → result=[0,2,5]. i=4: is_prime(4)=False → current_sum=5, append 5 → result=[0,2,5,5]. Output [0,2,5,5].

n=5, primes are 2,3,5. Initialize result=[], current_sum=0. i=1: not prime → append 0 → [0]. i=2: prime → current_sum=2 → append 2 → [0,2]. i=3: prime → current_sum=5 → append 5 → [0,2,5]. i=4: not prime → append 5 → [0,2,5,5]. i=5: prime → current_sum=10 → append 10 → [0,2,5,5,10]. Output [0,2,5,5,10].

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating and Checking Conditions
ladders.desc: Loop through a range and perform an action based on a condition.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating over a sequence of numbers and executing code conditionally. The intuition is to process each element in order and make decisions dynamically. Step-by-step: Define a range from start to end (e.g., 1 to n). For each value in the range, evaluate a condition (e.g., whether the number is prime). Based on the condition, perform an action (e.g., add to a sum). This is fundamental to many algorithms. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop, plus the cost of the condition check. Space complexity is O(1) for loop variables. Connection to L2: This is the loop structure used to accumulate sums conditionally. Common mistakes include incorrect range boundaries (off-by-one), modifying the loop variable inside the loop, or not initializing variables before the loop. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, n+1): if condition(i): do_something(i).

examples.input: n=3, condition: i>1, action: print i
examples.trace: n=3. Loop i from 1 to 3. i=1: condition i>1? 1>1 is False → do nothing. i=2: condition 2>1 is True → print 2. i=3: condition 3>1 is True → print 3. Output: 2 and 3 printed.

examples.input: n=5, condition: i%2==0, action: add i to sum (initial sum=0)

testCases.input: n=0, condition: True, action: count (initial count=0)
n=1, condition: i==1, action: set flag=True
n=2, condition: i<2, action: add i to list
n=3, condition: i%2==0, action: increment counter (start=0)
n=4, condition: i>2, action: multiply product (start=1)
n=5, condition: i in [2,3,5], action: add to sum (start=0)
n=6, condition: i>=4, action: append to list
n=7, condition: i<5, action: set max=i
n=9, condition: i is prime (primes up to 9: 2,3,5,7), action: add to sum (start=0)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Division Check
ladders.desc: Determine if one number is divisible by another using the modulo operator.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation checks divisibility by computing the remainder when one number is divided by another. The intuition is that if a number x is divisible by d, then x % d == 0. The modulo operator (%) returns the remainder of division. Step-by-step: Given two integers x and d, compute x % d. If the result is 0, then d divides x evenly; otherwise, it does not. This is a constant-time operation, O(1), assuming fixed-size integers. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in the condition check for primality testing (e.g., checking if i divides x). Common mistakes include confusing modulo with integer division, or not handling d=0 (which causes division by zero error). Real code pattern: if x % d == 0: print('divisible') else: print('not divisible').

examples.trace: Compute 6 % 2. 6 divided by 2 is 3 with remainder 0. So 6 % 2 = 0. Since remainder is 0, output True (divisible).
Compute 7 % 3. 7 divided by 3 is 2 with remainder 1 (since 3*2=6, 7-6=1). So 7 % 3 = 1. Since remainder is not 0, output False (not divisible).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Integer Loops and Ranges
ladders.desc: Iterate through a sequence of integers using a for-loop.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves using a loop to repeat an action for each integer in a specified range. The intuition is to automate repetitive tasks over a set of numbers. Step-by-step: Define a start and end integer. Use a for-loop to iterate from start to end-1 (inclusive of start, exclusive of end) or start to end (inclusive) depending on language. In each iteration, the loop variable takes on the next integer value, and the loop body executes. This is fundamental to processing collections of items. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of iterations. Space complexity is O(1) for the loop variable. Connection to L4: This loop structure is used to apply the modulo check repeatedly across numbers. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in range boundaries, infinite loops if modifying the loop variable, or confusion between zero-based and one-based indexing. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, 5): print(i) prints 1,2,3,4.

examples.trace: Using range(1,4) which includes start=1 and goes up to but not including 4. Iteration 1: i=1, print 1. Iteration 2: i=2, print 2. Iteration 3: i=3, print 3. Output: 1,2,3 printed.
Using range(0,3). Iteration 1: i=0, print 0. Iteration 2: i=1, print 1. Iteration 3: i=2, print 2. Output: 0,1,2 printed.

approaches.name: Sieve of Eratosthenes with Prefix Sum O(n log log n)
approaches.summary: Use the Sieve of Eratosthenes to generate all primes up to n, then compute prefix sums in a single pass. This is efficient for medium to large n by leveraging prime generation.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Compute Prime Prefix Sums up to n
ladders.desc: Given an integer n, compute the sum of all prime numbers ≤ n using prefix sums after generating primes via Sieve of Eratosthenes.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires efficiently computing the sum of all prime numbers up to a given integer n. The naive approach of checking each number for primality would be O(n√n), which is too slow for large n (e.g., n=10^6). The Sieve of Eratosthenes provides an optimal way to generate all primes up to n in O(n log log n) time. Once we have a boolean array marking primes, we can compute prefix sums in a single O(n) pass. The intuition is that by iteratively marking multiples of each prime as non-prime, we eliminate redundant primality checks. The prefix sum array allows O(1) queries for any range sum of primes up to a given value. Step-by-step: 1. Create boolean array is_prime[0..n] initialized to True (except 0 and 1). 2. For i from 2 to √n: if is_prime[i] is True, mark all multiples j = i*i, i*i+i, ... ≤ n as False. 3. Create prefix_sum array of size n+1 initialized to 0. 4. For i from 0 to n: prefix_sum[i] = prefix_sum[i-1] + (i if is_prime[i] else 0). 5. Return prefix_sum[n]. Time: O(n log log n) for sieve + O(n) for prefix sum = O(n log log n). Space: O(n) for both arrays. Connection to L1: This uses the sieve generation from L1 and adds prefix sum computation. Common mistakes: Not handling n<2 correctly (should return 0), using O(n) memory for sieve when n is large (use bytearray), or incorrect loop bounds in sieve. Real code pattern: def prime_prefix_sum(n): if n < 2: return 0; is_prime = [True]*(n+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i, n+1, i): is_prime[j]=False; prefix = [0]*(n+1); for i in range(2, n+1): prefix[i]=prefix[i-1]+(i if is_prime[i] else 0); return prefix[n]

examples.trace: Step 1: Generate primes up to 10 using sieve. Initialize is_prime[0..10] = [F,F,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T]. Mark multiples: i=2 → mark 4,6,8,10 as F → [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,T,F]; i=3 → mark 9 as F → [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,F,F]; i=4 not prime, skip; i=5 > √10, stop. Primes: 2,3,5,7. Step 2: Compute prefix sums: prefix[0]=0; prefix[1]=0; prefix[2]=2; prefix[3]=5; prefix[4]=5; prefix[5]=10; prefix[6]=10; prefix[7]=17; prefix[8]=17; prefix[9]=17; prefix[10]=17. Step 3: Return prefix[10]=17.

Step 1: Generate primes up to 5. is_prime[0..5] = [F,F,T,T,T,T]. i=2 → mark 4 as F → [F,F,T,T,F,T]; i=3 > √5, stop. Primes: 2,3,5. Step 2: Prefix sums: prefix[0]=0; prefix[1]=0; prefix[2]=2; prefix[3]=5; prefix[4]=5; prefix[5]=10. Step 3: Return prefix[5]=10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate Primes up to n using Sieve of Eratosthenes
ladders.desc: Implement the Sieve of Eratosthenes to efficiently list all prime numbers ≤ n.
ladders.explanation: The Sieve of Eratosthenes is an ancient algorithm for finding all primes up to a given limit n. The intuition is to iteratively mark the multiples of each prime starting from 2 as composite (non-prime), leaving the unmarked numbers as primes. This works because any composite number must have a prime factor ≤ its square root. By starting with the smallest prime (2) and marking its multiples, we ensure that when we reach a number that hasn't been marked, it must be prime (since all smaller primes have already processed their multiples). Step-by-step: 1. Create boolean array is_prime[0..n], initialize all to True except is_prime[0] and is_prime[1] = False. 2. For i from 2 to √n (inclusive): if is_prime[i] is True, then for j from i*i to n with step i: set is_prime[j] = False. 3. The remaining True values in is_prime are primes. Time: O(n log log n) – the sum of harmonic series of primes. Space: O(n) for the boolean array. Connection to L0: This generates the prime list needed for prefix sum computation. Common mistakes: Starting j from i*2 instead of i*i (redundant marking), not including √n in loop, or using O(n²) memory. Real code pattern: def sieve(n): if n < 2: return []; is_prime = [True]*(n+1); is_prime[0]=is_prime[1]=False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if is_prime[i]: for j in range(i*i, n+1, i): is_prime[j]=False; return [i for i, prime in enumerate(is_prime) if prime]

examples.trace: Initialize is_prime[0..10] = [F,F,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T,T]. i=2 (prime): mark multiples 4,6,8,10 → [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,T,F]. i=3 (prime): mark multiple 9 → [F,F,T,T,F,T,F,T,F,F,F]. i=4 not prime, skip. i=5 > √10, stop. Collect True indices: 2,3,5,7.
is_prime[0..20] all True except 0,1. i=2: mark 4,6,8,10,12,14,16,18,20. i=3: mark 9,15 (12,18 already marked). i=4 not prime. i=5 > √20, stop. Primes: indices with True: 2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mark Multiples of a Prime as Composite
ladders.desc: Given a prime p and limit n, mark all multiples of p (starting from p²) as non-prime in a boolean array.
ladders.explanation: This is the heart of the Sieve algorithm. For a prime number p, we know that all multiples of p (except p itself) are composite. The key insight is to start marking from p² instead of 2p, because any smaller multiple k*p where k < p would have already been marked by a smaller prime (since k must have a prime factor ≤ k < p). This optimization reduces redundant work. For example, for p=5, we start at 25 instead of 10, because 10=2*5 was already marked when processing p=2. Step-by-step: 1. Input: prime p, limit n, boolean array is_prime. 2. For j from p*p to n with step p: set is_prime[j] = False. 3. Continue until j > n. Time: O(n/p) for each prime p, which aggregates to O(n log log n). Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L1: This operation is repeated for each prime in the sieve loop. Common mistakes: Starting from 2p (inefficient), using while loop without step optimization, or not checking p² ≤ n before looping. Real code pattern: def mark_multiples(p, n, is_prime): start = p*p; if start > n: return; for j in range(start, n+1, p): is_prime[j] = False

examples.trace: Start at p²=4. Mark multiples: j=4 → is_prime[4]=F; j=6 → F; j=8 → F; j=10 → F. Result: indices 4,6,8,10 become False.
Start at p²=9. Mark multiples: j=9 → F; j=12 → F (but already False from p=2); j=15 → F; j=18 → F. Result: indices 9,15,18 become False (12 already False).

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through Numbers with Step Size
ladders.desc: Loop through a range of numbers with a fixed increment (step), e.g., all multiples of a given number.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental programming pattern used in the sieve to mark multiples. Instead of checking each number individually, we jump by a fixed step size to efficiently visit only the multiples. The intuition is that arithmetic progressions allow us to skip numbers that we know are irrelevant. For example, to mark multiples of 3 up to 30, we start at 3 and add 3 each time: 3,6,9,...,30. In the sieve, we start at p² for optimization. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize start value (e.g., p*p). 2. While start ≤ limit: process start, then start += step. 3. Use a for loop with range(start, limit+1, step) in Python. Time: O((limit-start)/step) linear in number of multiples. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This implements the marking of multiples. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop bounds, using floating-point steps, or infinite loops with while. Real code pattern: for j in range(start, end+1, step): # process j

examples.trace: j=4; process; j=6; process; j=8; process; j=10; process; stop as next j=12 > end.
j=9; process; j=12; process; j=15; process; j=18; process; stop as next j=21 > end.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check and Update Boolean Array Elements
ladders.desc: Access and modify elements in a boolean array, e.g., set is_prime[i] = False.
ladders.explanation: Arrays are fundamental data structures that store elements in contiguous memory. A boolean array holds True/False values. In the sieve, we use an array is_prime where index i represents number i, and is_prime[i] indicates whether i is prime. The operation involves: 1. Accessing is_prime[i] to check if it's True (meaning i is potentially prime). 2. Updating is_prime[j] = False to mark j as composite. This is O(1) time per access/update. The intuition is that arrays provide direct indexing, allowing efficient lookups and modifications. Step-by-step: To mark multiples: for j in multiples: is_prime[j] = False. To check primality: if is_prime[i]: # i is prime. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(n) for the array. Connection to L3: This is the action performed in the step iteration. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds (e.g., accessing is_prime[n] when size is n), confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based numbers, or not initializing properly. Real code pattern: is_prime = [True]*(n+1); is_prime[0] = False; if is_prime[i]: # do something; is_prime[j] = False

examples.input: Initialize is_prime size 6, set indices 0,1,4 to False
examples.trace: Create array of 6 True: [T,T,T,T,T,T]. Set is_prime[0]=F → [F,T,T,T,T,T]. Set is_prime[1]=F → [F,F,T,T,T,T]. Set is_prime[4]=F → [F,F,T,T,F,T].
Access is_prime[2] → True; is_prime[3] → True; is_prime[4] → False.

testCases.input: size=10, all T, set F at even indices

testCases.expected: [False, True, True, True, True, True, False]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Prime Numbers and Multiples
ladders.desc: Define prime numbers and identify multiples of a given number.
ladders.explanation: A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. For example, 2,3,5,7 are primes. A composite number has more than two divisors, like 4 (divisible by 1,2,4). The intuition is that primes are the building blocks of integers via prime factorization. A multiple of a number p is any number that can be expressed as p*k for some integer k. For example, multiples of 3 are 3,6,9,12,... This is foundational because the sieve relies on marking multiples of primes as composite. Step-by-step to check if n is prime: try dividing by integers from 2 to √n; if any division has remainder 0, n is composite. To find multiples of p up to limit m: list all p, 2p, 3p, ... ≤ m. Time: O(√n) for primality check, O(m/p) for listing multiples. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Boolean arrays mark numbers as prime/composite based on this understanding. Common mistakes: Thinking 1 is prime, or not including the number itself as a multiple. Real code pattern: def is_prime(n): if n<2: return False; for i in range(2, int(n**0.5)+1): if n%i==0: return False; return True

examples.trace: n=7. Check divisors: 2 (7%2=1 ≠0), 3 (7%3=1), 4 (>√7≈2.6, stop). No divisors found, so prime.
